specification.topic-statement: The debug log file system provides compile-time-conditional printf-style diagnostic logging to a rotating plain-text file.

scope.in-scope: Compile-time gating of all logging behavior, log directory creation, rotation of any pre-existing log file on startup, log file creation, per-call write format, immediate flushing, and atexit cleanup.
scope.boundary: The application's persistent structured logger records events in JSONL format to a different file path and is always active regardless of build mode. This topic is entirely separate from that logger.

If any initialization step fails (missing environment variable, failed directory creation, failed file open), the panic handler is invoked and the process terminates. The panic handler's behavior is out of scope here.

field.name: timestamp
field.note: local time formatted as YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS

field.name: source file
field.note: the source file path as provided by the compiler

field.name: function name
field.note: the enclosing function name as provided by the compiler

field.name: timestamp portion
field.note: birth time (or mtime fallback) of the previous log file, formatted as YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SSZ±HH-MM using local timezone offset

behavior.compile-time-gating:
All logging behavior — initialization, the write call, and the public macro — is present only in DEBUG builds. In non-DEBUG builds, both the initialization call and every log-write call compile to no-ops that produce no code and have no side effects. There is no runtime flag or environment variable that enables or disables the feature; the build configuration is the sole switch.
The source file is wrapped in LCOV coverage exclusion directives, so coverage tooling does not measure any of its lines.
behavior.initialization:
Initialization must be called once early in the process lifecycle before any log writes are attempted.
The log directory path is read from an environment variable. If the variable is unset or empty, the process panics immediately.
If the path does not yet exist as a directory, it is created recursively with mode 0755. Any intermediate directories that do not exist are also created. Creation proceeds segment by segment; if a segment already exists, that is not treated as an error. If creation fails for any other reason, the process panics.
If a file named "debug.log" already exists in the log directory, it is archived before the new log is opened:
1. The birth time of the existing file is queried using the statx system call with the STATX_BTIME flag. If the kernel does not support birth time or does not report it for this file, the file's modification time is used as a fallback.
2. The birth time is converted to local time and formatted as a timestamp string with the local timezone offset, using hyphens as separators within the time portion to produce a filesystem-safe name of the form YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS±HH-MM.
3. An archive path is constructed by appending ".log" to the timestamp string within the log directory.
4. If no file at the archive path already exists, the existing "debug.log" is renamed to the archive path. If the archive path already exists, the rename is skipped and "debug.log" is left in place to be overwritten.
If the archive file already exists — which can happen if two processes start within the same second — the existing log is overwritten by the new session rather than being preserved or merged. The collision check uses a second stat call; there is a TOCTOU race between that check and the rename.
A new "debug.log" file is opened for writing in the log directory, truncating any file that was not renamed during the rotation step. If the open fails, the process panics.
A cleanup function is registered with atexit to flush and close the file when the process exits normally.
A header line reading "=== IKIGAI DEBUG LOG ===" is written to the new file and flushed immediately.
behavior.log-write:
Each log write emits one line to the file. The public interface is a macro that automatically supplies the source file path, line number, and function name at the call site; the caller supplies only the format string and any arguments.
The write sequence is: 1. If the log file is not open (not yet initialized, or already closed by the atexit handler), the write is silently discarded. 2. The current local time is obtained and formatted as YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS. 3. A context prefix is written: "[TIMESTAMP] FILE:LINE:FUNCTION: " 4. The caller's formatted message is appended using printf-style formatting. 5. A newline character is appended. 6. The file is flushed immediately so the file can be tailed in real time.
Every log call flushes the underlying file stream. This enables real-time observation via tail -f but imposes a flush cost on every log statement.
behavior.cleanup:
The atexit handler flushes and closes the log file, then sets the internal file pointer to null. If the file pointer is already null when the handler runs, it does nothing.
The atexit handler is registered once at initialization. It cannot be unregistered. If initialization is called more than once (which is not a supported usage), multiple handlers would be registered and would each attempt to flush and close whatever the file pointer points to at exit time.
